lenses.guidance: Pharaoh's daughter names the child Moses 'because I drew him out of the water' (v 10) — Hebrew mashah, 'to draw out'. The Egyptian princess speaks Hebrew etymology; the original audience hears the deliverer's name pun on deliverance itself.

The 'basket' Moses is placed in (v 3) is Hebrew tebah — the same word used only of Noah's ark (Gen 6:14). The narrative repetition signals: God saves a people through a saved deliverer floating on the same word.

Moses is preserved from genocide (vv 1-10), drawn from water (v 10), and grows up to lead his people out of Egypt — the pattern Matthew 2:13-15 explicitly applies to Christ saved from Herod and called out of Egypt.

God 'remembered his covenant with Abraham' (v 24) — Hebrew zakar, used elsewhere of Noah (Gen 8:1) and Hannah (1 Sam 1:19). The redemptive arc moves when God 'remembers'; deliverance follows the verb.

Moses' first attempt at deliverance fails — he kills, hides, flees (vv 11-15). Forty years in Midian precede the call. Pray when self-effort has collapsed; God's deliverers are shaped by their failures, not exempted from them.
